items.title: Pre-register hypotheses and α
items.avoid: Trying many comparisons and highlighting only those with p < 0.05.
items.prefer: State H₀, H₁, test, α, and primary metric before running experiments; apply multiple-comparison correction when testing many metrics.
items.why: Uncontrolled fishing inflates Type I errors—especially common when sweeping prompts, temperatures, or model sizes.
items.example: Testing 20 prompt variants at α=0.05 without correction expects ~1 false "winner" by chance alone.

items.title: Report effect size and uncertainty, not only p
items.avoid: Publishing "p = 0.03" with no mean difference or confidence interval.
items.prefer: Pair p-values with point estimates and CIs (e.g. "success rate +3.2%, 95% CI [0.4%, 6.0%], p = 0.03").
items.why: Decision-makers need magnitude and precision; significance does not imply practical importance.
items.example: F1 +0.008, p=0.02 on 10k examples may be statistically significant but too small to justify a costly model swap.

items.title: Match test to data structure
items.avoid: Using independent two-sample t-tests on paired before/after measurements.
items.prefer: Use paired t-tests for matched units, Welch for unequal variances, proportion z-tests for rates, and check independence of observations.
items.why: Wrong pairing or variance assumptions distort the reference distribution and p-values.
items.example: Same 100 users rated Model A then B → paired differences; `ttest_rel` not `ttest_ind`.

items.title: Plan sample size for power
items.avoid: Running until p < 0.05 (optional stopping) or using whatever n is convenient.
items.prefer: Estimate required n from expected effect size, σ, desired power (e.g. 0.8), and α before the experiment.
items.why: Underpowered tests waste compute and routinely fail to reject real improvements.
items.example: To detect a 5% success-rate lift from 70% with power 0.8 at α=0.05, you need roughly 350+ per arm—not 30.
